Transcrição Completa: How To Recruit Top Sales Reps
[00:00] So, first of all, just a quick side note before we begin. In the weekly inner circle group chat library on your training courses here, the video that I was just talking about that I made, Vlad, I added it in and dated it for yesterday. So, that's there. It's also in our Telegram chat. Also, just on another side note, there's the inner circle mastermind talks now that are available.
[00:30] I made an announcement about this yesterday in our chat as well, but just to be clear, we have this entire training library of all the historical masterminds that were recorded with the camera in the back of the room. We have the footage from like 2021 and 2020 and 2019, but those were all like the meeting footage, kind of like this one as an example. It just kind of looked like shit. So, long story short, we just went with the ones that were 2022 onward. That way you guys have everything that actually looked good.
[01:00] Okay. So, today we're talking about how to recruit top sales reps. We're going to start by going through a very very important lesson. And this is important to understand. Okay. So, first of all, we have a tremendous amount of lessons here that we're going to go through today on what top reps want, what top reps need when they come into an offer, and then how to find them, how to recruit them, you know, how to communicate to them, and all that fun stuff.
[01:30] So, let's start off with the goods. Okay. Number one, when you recruit any top rep, okay, any top closer is what we'll call them. And you could apply the same logic to setters, by the way. We start off with, well, what do they want to get paid? Throw into the chat, what do you think top closers want to get paid? What do you think the magic number is? It's okay to have a range, by the way. Let's see.
[02:00] 20K, 15K, 20 to 30K. Yeah. Well, this makes sense why none of you guys are attracting top reps. See, Kaden, on the other hand, Kaden, unmute yourself real quick. Kaden runs a fucking sales agency and literally recruits, retains, and staffs top closers for businesses routinely. Kaden, what was your number?
[02:15] "40,000. And I even pushed it higher to like 50 if I'm being completely honest."
[02:20] Yeah, that's exactly right. Fucking bingo. That's what top reps really want. Okay, most of you guys think that they want to make fucking $100,000 a year. Are you guys like oblivious to the fact that 100k a year is like no fucking money now? Like people get made fun of for making $10,000 a month? You think a fucking closer wants to make a measly $10,000 a month? Fucking no.
[02:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Top Closers Want To Get Paid"
NÚMEROS: $10K/month, $20K/month, $40K-50K/month
VISUAL: Comparação de valores monetários em escala crescente, destaque visual para o range de $40-50K como objetivo real dos top closers
[03:00] You know, and 20k a month, that's 240k a year. Sure that's doing better and like it's definitely obviously double what 10K is and they're feeling all right if maybe their first month or their second month is you know somewhere in the range of 20K but it's like in reality these guys want to be on the upward side of the spectrum here of making about 50k a month okay Leo unmute yourself what are you going to make this month?
[03:30] "I believe we should be close to almost like 35K."
[03:35] Bang. How long you been working with me?
[03:38] "Close to a decade, if not already a decade."
[03:42] I think it has been a decade. By the way, thank you for your service.
[03:45] "Of course."
[03:47] See, now Leo gets paid, dog. Leo gets paid buku bucks, okay? For a standard person. Think about this. And this is also important to note. It's like, all right, then you got to ask, well, what do they want? Okay, what does a top closer actually want? And there's a few things that you got to consider.
[04:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Top Closers Want"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Lista de elementos-chave: Money (dinheiro em primeiro lugar), Personal Development (desenvolvimento pessoal como segundo item principal)
[04:15] First of all, well, let's actually just not posture here. Money. Okay. Second thing, personal development. And this one's huge. So, one big reason that you can retain a top closer is being a top closer is like a personal development journey. It requires you to go through a very consistent routine where there is significant levels of accountability.
[04:45] And this is all kind of tied together with this, you know, personal development characteristic here. It's like, again, I'm not trying to posture like sound fucking cheesy here or just make shit up. Like this is something that top closers consistently value. Most people who go out and become business owners, it's fucking hard. Okay? Otherwise, everybody would do it.
[05:15] The level of stress that a business owner endures compared to like a top rep. Like, dude, top reps, they just have to close. Like, they have to update a fucking CRM, you know? They have to follow up with some people, but like for the most part, like they get to define their schedule, their availability, how much money they make is within their own control.
[05:45] It's like, you know, they're essentially shielded from all of the extreme stress that comes with running the business, but they still want the upside of the most lucrative position to be in within a company, which is again being a top closer. Okay, top closers get paid. They get paid the most and they should get paid the most out of anybody else within that organization.
[06:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Top Closers Do NOT Want Business Owner Stress"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Contraste visual entre estresse de dono de negócio vs. vida de closer, ícones representando proteção/escudo contra estresse, destaque para separação clara de responsabilidades
[06:15] But they do not, okay, and this is very critical. They do not want business owner stress. Okay, this is critical. You got to shield them from this. Leo, unmute yourself. Be honest with the people. Do you deal with any stress that I have to deal with in this organization?
[06:30] "No, not at all."
[06:32] Completely shielded from it. Do you think I endure a fuckload of stress?
[06:35] "Yeah, for sure."
[06:37] Yeah. Okay. Now, be honest with people, okay? It's okay to say yes if it's true. Do you want to swap with me? Like, would you want to be me after you've seen over a decade what it takes to be me?
[06:50] "No. I still can't fathom like how you do what you do still, even year after year, you know."
[06:58] Dude. Look at what you've got to do in the meantime. It's like, how many kids you got?
[07:02] "I've got four now."
[07:04] You like having a family?
[07:06] "Oh, yeah. Love it."
[07:08] What do you get to do that I don't get to do?
[07:10] "Spend more time with them."
[07:12] Dude, you get to like you put how many miles on that fucking car that the company gives you?
[07:18] "Like 30,000 in two years."
[07:21] Dude, Leo's put 30,000 miles on a vehicle that we provided him as a company. Okay. What are you driving around doing with 30,000 miles, Leo?
[07:30] "Family stuff, man. You know, after school stuff."
[07:33] Leo goes on trips. Okay. Leo goes on vacations. Leo, by the way, we're happy to fund these things for Leo, too, man. Like what because Leo provides for the organization and we appreciate what he does, how long he's been with us. Like we reward the fuck out of him, you know?
[07:55] So here's my point, okay? They want to be shielded from the business owner stress, okay? Like openly they'll admit like I don't want that fucking role. But you know the role that they do want, they want the greatest role. I tell Leo this like way back in the day. Leo, you remember like couple days into the business, I said, "Dude, this is like the greatest fucking role in the business because you just get to do fucking deals all day and then you don't have to deal with any of the operational shit or like anything that comes along with the fulfillment. Your sole job is to just close deals." Like, is that not the best job in the fucking world or what?
[08:35] "Yeah, I remember that."
[08:37] Yeah, dude. Okay, my point being this role has to be created in a way where they have a high probability to get 20K at least to 50K plus a month. Okay? And this has to be consistent. We have to find a way to load up calendars, provide leads.
[08:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Creating The Role: Requirements"
NÚMEROS: $20K-$50K+/month consistently
VISUAL: Checklist visual com itens: calendários cheios, leads fornecidos, sistema provado, resultados comprovados, processo definido
[09:00] This isn't mandatory, I want to be clear, but it sure does fucking help when you're trying to attract top closers. You got to have a system that's already proven, like something that already works that you're just plugging people into. Okay? Most top closers, like they don't want to come into chaos. And chaos is the equivalent of having an undefined system that does not have any proven outcomes.
[09:30] Okay? Like sure, if it's one of your first reps that you've ever hired, you know, there's going to be some development of course, like there's going to be some routines that are established together, undone when you find out they don't work. You know, there's going to be like a higher frequency of calls initially that you might do and then you find out you don't need to do that many and you kind of scale it back.
[10:00] And then for all the reps that come in after that, you know, you know exactly what to do and exactly what works and exactly how to plug them in and yada yada. The point I'm trying to make is pretty simple. You have to understand that when I say a proven system that mitigates chaos, it's like when you think back to this real quick with me, guys, okay?
[10:25] Last time you worked a job, imagine coming into the job and feeling the uncertainty of the business, like just like the uncertainty weight of the business on your shoulders. Like I remember my very first job I ever had worked at a grocery store. I felt like I just immediately got plugged into a system. It was like effortless, efficient. It just felt like how you should get onboarded into a job.
[10:50] You know, there were people available to me for me to ask questions to. There were consistent daily tasks that I was clearly told I needed to repeat and do and execute. There was like a management structure. There was accountability just with clocking in and clocking out, you know.
[11:10] My second job working selling direct TV inside of different retailers. Okay, that was my first commission sales job. Same thing. I had a specific store for that day that I would go to. I had specific people that I could communicate with when I needed training help or if I had questions. I had in that case because it was a sales job, proper sales management that would actually fucking look over what I was doing and correct me when I'm wrong and like tell me the things that I could do to improve upon.
[11:40] Okay? And more importantly than anything else, I had like a development program in that business that brought potential out of me. Okay? They always paid on time. I never had any issues with, you know, again, getting money from these businesses or having like, you know, some kind of problems like that. The point I'm trying to make is like that's a great example of mitigating chaos.
[12:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mitigating Chaos: The Proven System"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado - CAOS (sistema indefinido, sem resultados provados) vs. SISTEMA PROVADO (onboarding estruturado, management claro, accountability, desenvolvimento, pagamento consistente)
[12:15] Okay. So, first of all, I just want you to answer in the chat with a simple yes or no. Can you currently make a sales rep 20 to $50,000 a month? Yes or no? Okay? And this is based on how you currently have things. So, it's like 20 to $50,000 a month. Honest answer. Can you actually make a rep that much money?
[12:40] Okay? Because if you can't, well, that's where your problem starts. Okay, we got more than half of you saying no. You see, and like, dude, you don't stand a fucking chance to attract top closers, and that's why you won't. It's like, you have to start with, can I make this person that amount, okay? And you have to find a way to make that possible.
[13:05] It's not like some obscenely hard thing to do. It's not like I'm telling you you need to bump your commissions up to 50%. Okay? I'm gonna have a few of you unmute yourself and tell me why you can't. Max, you answered no initially. Unmute, please.
[13:20] "Yeah, I don't charge enough. I mean, there's no—"
[13:23] Why can you not make 20 to 50k a month for a rep?
[13:26] "Yeah, there's no shot. I mean, if the program is 1,600 bucks, like there's just no shot's going to happen. I would need to charge 10, 15, possibly even more to make that reasonable with the commission structure that I have."
[13:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Pricing Problem"
NÚMEROS: $1,600 atual vs. $10K-$15K necessário
VISUAL: Seta ascendente dramática mostrando gap de preço, cálculo visual de comissões, comparação de modelos de negócio
[13:45] See, fucking bingo. So, it's like right when you start to consider how could I pay a 20 to 50k a month rep. You see the do you see the prompts that you're essentially faced with that force you to reconsider your model? It's like right now you're running a selfish little pay your rent model. You know, you're not fucking actually collecting any serious cash here and getting richer and getting other people richer.
[14:15] What's the point of a company, Max? Right? It's like well obviously we want to service the customers and the clients that we have successfully but more importantly we want to enrich everybody that's a part of the organization. So when we look to bring in top talent that's like the number one prerogative. What has to change to support more than just you?
[14:40] Okay. What has to develop in the business change? What has to be tweaked to find that specific way of operating that actually attracts everybody that you want, not just these top reps, but everybody that you need to financially support, right? You want A players in the business through and through. You're going to fucking dish out the big bucks. You get what I mean?
[15:05] Okay. Point being, that prompt right away of I'd have to take my pricing up. Well, we had to do the same thing. I remember back in the day, Leo, you remember like the literal first days of the agency when we were charging like 1,800 bucks a month for social media shit and then what did we do within the first like I don't know it's like fucking two weeks of running that thing.
[15:30] "Yeah, we bumped up to four grand right away."
[15:33] Why? Just because we had people that need to get paid, you know, and then we were like, "Fuck selling this. Like, let's start selling things that are like eight grand, 12 grand, you know, and we just started pushing up." Why? Because we wanted people to get paid. We wanted to enrich the people participating in the organization.
[15:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pricing Evolution: Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $1,800/month → $4,000 (2 weeks) → $8K-$12K
VISUAL: Timeline visual mostrando evolução de pricing, razão principal destacada: "Pay People", gráfico de crescimento acelerado
[16:00] Okay. So, Max, going from 1600 to 10 to 15. What is that? Is that even possible? Can you do that? You have to change anything else?
[16:10] "Yeah, I'd have to change a bunch of stuff, bro. I mean, I think—"
[16:13] What would you have to change? Walk through it. So, price goes up. What else would you have to do? Deliverables change? Does the result you're going after change? Does the demographic change? Like, what is it?
[16:25] "I think the messaging changes for sure. So, it's targeting more rich people. I think the conversion mechanism would need to change to build more trust. The deliverables would probably need to change a little bit. We already do a lot of done for you stuff, but we could do even more for them. And yeah, I mean it would have to be a major change to get someone to pay 10-15k, you know? It'd be huge."
[16:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Changes With Higher Pricing"
NÚMEROS: $1,600 → $10K-$15K transformation
VISUAL: Checklist de mudanças necessárias: Messaging (target rico), Conversion Mechanism (mais trust), Deliverables (mais done-for-you), Demographics (upgrade de cliente)
[16:55] Bingo. Okay. Now these see what I mean? Like when you take on a task that forces your organization to improve, is that not the best thing for that organization to take on as a challenge? Like think about it. Taking on a top rep immediately forces a proper shift that makes the organization more money and puts you in a better financial position. Those are some of the best challenges you can take on.
[17:25] Okay, let's continue on. Mo, I know why you can't take on 20 to 50K reps yet. You know, you just got to get more deals. I understand that fully. Yan, unmute yourself. So, you just bumped the price up to 5Gs. Why can't you get a 20 or 50k rep?
[17:45] "Yeah. So, when I'm looking at the numbers, if it all checks out, let's say we do like a $100 cost per call. And I looked at the numbers in a sheet, we would be able to for sure. But at the current rate, like it's just like I said, it's day one. And I'm not going to sit here and lie to you and say yes. Like right now, we've not actually run ads to that sales page I sent you yet. That's just from organic. So, it's just too early. But with the numbers, if they do check out, we can. So, yeah."
[18:15] This is good. Now, you and I'm going to ask you a good question here. When is the best time for you to try to bring in a rep?
[18:25] "So, right now it's outsourced, which I know you don't like. We have got kind of like a guy managing it through kind of his own sales department. There's two closes already. Ideally though, like if we want to be hitting like 200 PIFFs in Jan, we would obviously need more than two closers. So ideally getting the gears in motion, which I've already spoke to Dev who's kind of managing it and he's looking at getting a new closer like ASAP. He's training him up on the offer. But yeah, we'll probably likely need to take it a bit slower to kind of see where we're at just because again there's not enough closes there."
[19:00] Be a good politician. You answer questions extremely indirectly. Allow me to answer it for you. When is the best time to take on a rep? After you already have a proven system.
[19:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When To Hire Reps: The Golden Rule"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Timeline clara mostrando: PRIMEIRO - Sistema Provado (você fazendo calls, aprendendo o que funciona), DEPOIS - Contratar Reps (plugar no sistema existente)
[19:15] So Euan needs to get his calls maxed the fuck out. He needs to figure out what specifically works on the phones. It's like Andrew, unmute yourself real quick. How many calls a day were you taking before you hired on reps? You're muted. Or you're not muted. It doesn't show you're muted, but we can't hear you.
[19:35] "It was seven or eight a day."
[19:38] And then how long did you do that before you eventually hired a rep?
[19:42] "Too long. Like six months."
[19:45] So you went well over the threshold of doing it yourself, making money that way to be fair. But the main threshold in that 6 months, when did you officially cross a barrier where you said, "I'm confident enough at this point to know what works and what doesn't work on these calls. I should get off the phones and try to replace myself."
[20:05] "It took me I mean, I was booked out over a week. That's when I knew if I got sick, which happened like I got COVID in August, I lost revenue. That's when I knew like I needed somebody else."
[20:18] At what threshold did you just feel confident you could trade in somebody else based on what you figured out worked and what didn't work?
[20:26] "I mean I probably took 500 calls before I was like okay I need to get somebody else in here and I'm confident in our process."
[20:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The 500 Call Threshold"
NÚMEROS: 7-8 calls/day, 6 months total, 500 calls = confidence point
VISUAL: Gráfico de progresso mostrando acúmulo de calls até ponto de confiança, indicador de "ready to hire" em 500 calls (~2-2.5 meses)
[20:38] So 500 calls that would be what? Two months, two and a half months.
[20:42] "Well for me because it was all organic too. I didn't run ads until like September this year. I probably could have done it 90 days. I just went way too long."
[20:53] Did you feel there's a significant difference between organic and paid leads?
[20:57] "Hell yeah."
[20:59] Would there be value in you taking paid lead calls initially, learning that same process and then handing it off to closers just as you did with the organic?
[21:08] "Yes. And that's I stayed in. So, as we added in ads, I was filling up my closer calendar. There were still more than enough calls for me to get in there and figure things out before I added on even more closers."
[21:22] Bang. See, so the goal is, okay, I'm gonna give you guys some great perspective. One of the sales agencies I hate with all of my heart is two different people. Number one, Cole Gordon, and then there's another organization, a guy named Ambrose. And he sucks so bad he changed his business name because of how trashed his previous business was.
[21:45] To be clear, Cole Gordon specifically I dislike for different reasons. Ambrose has another set of reasons and I'm going to articulate both. These are both examples of outsourced sales agencies.
[22:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Outsourced Sales Agencies: The Problems"
NÚMEROS: 90%+ não fazem $10K/month (dado FTC sobre Cole Gordon)
VISUAL: Comparação de dois modelos problemáticos - Cole Gordon vs. Ambrose, ícones de alerta vermelho, problemas estruturais destacados
[22:05] So with Cole Gordon's offer, I can sit here and rant all day long, but the main thing that I dislike about it is the scale that Cole operates at. The scale that Cole operates at, there's no incentive for him to put good closers onto your account. How does it really work? Well, Cole sells people who are not closers on becoming a remote closer and getting a job that can pay 10K a month.
[22:35] That's what got him shut down by the FTC because 90 plus% of the people who bought his programs, without exaggeration, it was like 99% literally according to the FTC directly were not making even $10,000 a month.
[22:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cole Gordon Model Breakdown"
NÚMEROS: $15K upfront (business), $2K (closer training), 99% não fazem $10K/month
VISUAL: Fluxograma mostrando: Business paga $15K → Closer paga $2K → Training rápido → Closer fraco no account → Swap infinito de closers ruins, ciclo vicioso ilustrado
[22:55] So, here's what was happening. Cole would charge a business 15K upfront. He would charge a closer a couple grand. And then what would occur is he would take that closer who went through a training, a rapid training by the way, and dispatch them onto the business that paid him 15K. And the promise to the business was, hey, if this closer fails, I'll swap it out with another closer.
[23:25] So then what happens is, especially as this scales, the closers get worse and worse and worse because they're not getting the intimate attention that closers need. They're not getting the routine they need. And on top of that, he's not even articulating, hey, become a top closer. He's just saying make a measly fucking 10K a month, which as you all know now attracts not top closers. It attracts mediocre talent comparatively.
[23:55] I digress. At scale, this offer becomes problematic because simply put in its finest non-emotional way of articulating it. A business pays Cole 15K. He puts shit reps onto their account. There is no proper sales management in place to hold those closers accountable, develop them, train them. The business owner is not equipped with what they need to know or train the person on or do because they're omitting the responsibility of running the sales organization to Cole and his team.
[24:30] And then you have this totally backwards incentivized system where it just has a really dramatically high probability to fail. Okay.
[24:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ambrose Model: Even Worse"
NÚMEROS: 2 year contracts, $500K/month client stuck at 4 closers
VISUAL: Lista de problemas: No call recordings shared, No CRM data, No statistics, No transparency, Contract lock-in de 2 anos, ícone de corrente/prisão
[24:42] Now, if you move over to Ambrose's business, Ambrose, I'm not even familiar with what his current business name is because again, he failed with it enough to have to change the business name. I also want to disclose I've made enough money with Ambrose in the past on good deals we've done back when he was smaller and doing really well that he bought me a fucking AP like a $20-some thousand watch just to celebrate the success we had on deals.
[25:15] So historically I've done well with this guy but again that was when he was more intimate meaning he himself was more involved. He himself right just like Cole like is Cole a good closer? Absolutely. Is Cole a smart guy? Absolutely. Are these guys that I'm saying here like pieces of shit? No. It's just again like when you look at an outsourced sales system, the incentives are generally off.
[25:45] Okay, that's the main thing. It's like when they're smaller, they do really well. Like take Kaden and Trey. Like you guys don't hear me sitting here talking shit. I don't give a fuck if Kaden and Trey pay me money to be in the inner circle. If they did a bad job, I'd talk bad about them too. But Kaden and Trey is an example in the inner circle. They do good because they operate at that level of what actually has to get done in a sales organization to make it work successfully.
[26:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Good Sales Agencies vs. Bad"
NÚMEROS: $40K-$50K/month (Kaden's standard)
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado - GOOD (Kaden & Trey, Josh Troy/Wires from Strangers): proper management, top reps, transparency vs. BAD (Cole, Ambrose): scale problems, no management, bad incentives
[26:20] They do proper sales management. They actually hire top salespeople. Notice what Kaden said in the chat. By the way, as a proper sales agency, he's saying reps want to get paid 40k. They're not taking on little turd accounts. They're taking on accounts that can actually pay top reps that amount of money.
[26:45] Whereas again, like when you look at a model like what Ambrose's business is, it's awful in comparison. He hires like 20-some year old kids and he tries to replicate Cole's model where he tries to get them to buy a course. That's how he tries to farm reps. He then, this is the most unethical thing, by the way. There's plenty of unethical things. He'll generally contract people for up to two full years.
[27:15] Okay? In addition to that, I found this out just yesterday, by the way, on a sales call with a client that we wouldn't take on because they're working with Ambrose. They specifically said that if they try to request sales call recordings to review so they can help the closers be better, they don't provide them. They claim they have a company policy that they won't share call recordings to the business.
[27:45] Imagine how fucking shit you got to be to hire a sales team that does not share call recordings with you. Here's the craziest part, too. They don't share CRM data. They don't report any statistics. Tell me on this call right now, imagine not knowing your show rate or your close rate, and the closing team that you chose to work with says, "Oh, we have a company policy. We don't share any of that data."
[28:15] You know why they don't share that data? Because they're fucking awful. They are the worst people on earth to work with for sales agencies. Arguably worse than Cole Gordon now. And my point being is like truly imagine not knowing your show rate, not knowing your close rate, not being able to see your fucking CRM data for where people are at in different pipeline stages, not being able to look and review call recordings.
[28:45] And it's like, again, this is a sales agency. By the way, guys, listen to this. The client that wanted to work with us last night that we said no to, they were doing $500,000 a month. They're maxed out on calls. They have four closers. I said, "Why do you not have another four closers already?" And then they said, "Oh, we're working with Ambrose."
[29:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Ambrose Client Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $500K/month revenue, 4 closers, could be $1M/month with 8 closers
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando potencial perdido - receita atual vs. receita possível, seta bloqueada representando growth artificial limitado pela agência
[29:15] It's like, bingo. The sales agency could have just literally doubled the sales team. But they didn't. Why? Because listen to this. This is the craziest part. You ready? So the sales agency again remember they have to operate at a small level. The bigger they get the harder everything becomes. The bigger they get the more people they have to hire. The more people they have to hire the more communication lines there are. The more people there are the less effective they are.
[29:45] So if they doubled that person's revenue from 500k to a million meaning they double the reps from four to eight. Without exaggeration, if they did that just on a handful of clients that they actively manage and run, their business would dramatically increase in its probability to fail as a result.
[30:05] So what do they do instead? They just fucking hold the businesses back so they can essentially leech off of that revenue that they are able to create for that business, live a lifestyle, and not have to operate as serious business people that scale businesses. Instead, they hold businesses back. That's not exaggerated. That's not an emotional thing. That is as literal as it was in that exact scenario.
[30:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Outsourced Agencies Hold You Back"
NÚMEROS: 4 closers → 8 closers = 2x complexity = higher failure probability
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando: mais reps = mais comunicação = mais management = mais problemas = agência prefere manter pequeno e sugar revenue, ciclo de limitação intencional
[30:40] So, it's like again, sales agencies are generally absolutely like they're like the dumbest fucking thing you could use unless they're proper. There's only two sales agencies that I genuinely like. Okay? One of them is Kaden and Trey. Excuse me, Kaden, for not knowing your business name. And then two is Josh Troy, which I've brought in front of you guys with his company Wires from Strangers.
[31:05] And even then, just to be extremely clear, I don't encourage sales agencies. I encourage in-house sales teams, okay? Just like Andrew did.
[31:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "In-House Sales Team: The Right Way"
NÚMEROS: 500 calls = confidence threshold
VISUAL: Processo step-by-step: YOU do calls → Learn what works → Document SOPs → Build call library → Create training → THEN hire reps → Plug into proven system
[31:20] The whole point I'm trying to make with giving you guys these two different examples is I'm trying to give you perspective on what these sales agencies do versus, okay, what would you do in house? Well, here's how you would properly run an organization in house. You would first of all have a recruitment process that's simple and repeatable so you can bring in top reps and plug them into a system.
[31:45] What would the system be? Well, if you do calls yourself and you're aware of what works and what doesn't work, you immediately have the opportunity to then just plug somebody into a system and straight up tell them, "Hey, look man, here's a library of all these past call recordings I did. Here's the ones that worked. Here's the ones that didn't work. By the way, I have an entire set of SOPs and best practices that I've documented for you. Here's like the word tracks that have worked well. Here's like the best questions that have worked well, you know, and again, like you have all this material from taking the time to actually be in the trenches yourself as an operator and then replace yourself with top reps.
[32:25] Okay, that's the key. That is one of the most critical processes that you can actually go through. And most business owners will sit here and try to convince themselves like, "Oh, dude, I'm above having to take sales calls." No, you're not. That's fucking bullshit. That's one of the worst things you could do. Why? Because again, this is the gatekeeper of your revenue.
[32:50] If you're going to flood all your money through closers, like if you're going to get your money through salespeople, why in the world would you not want to be involved in that? Why in the world would you not want to maximize that specific revenue? That's like one of the most revenue-driven actions you could take is getting in the trenches, learning what works, and then getting yourself out of the trenches and having a repeatable system that you can just plug sales reps into.
[33:20] Okay? I view that as a very critical part of the process. Simply put. And because most business owners omit that responsibility and have the mindset that like they're above that, they typically just defer to these outsourced sales agencies at this point. So that's kind of the mental hurdle you have to overcome if you find yourself being more enticed by sales agencies.
[33:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Recruitment: Where To Find Top Closers"
NÚMEROS: Dezenas de milhares de reps em grupos do Facebook
VISUAL: Screenshot de busca no Facebook mostrando grupos: Dan Lok groups, job opportunities, closer communities, closer job boards
[33:50] Simply put, let's go into recruitment. Okay, so there's plenty of ways to recruit. Number one, you have all these Facebook groups. So, if you literally just go on Facebook, okay, so all I did here was go on Facebook and I typed in the word closer. Okay, I then navigated over here to the word groups and then I want you to look at this just literally by typing in the word closer. Watch what comes up straight away.
[34:20] Okay, so these are Dan Lock's groups. Whether you like Dan Lock or not, you know, whatever. You don't have to join these ones. You can join some other ones. So, it's like you want to look for these ones specifically that talk about job opportunities. Okay? You want to look for the ones that specifically talk about here's just job boards, closer opportunities, closer communities, you know, and if you look at these, like they have tens of thousands of reps in almost all instances, okay?
[34:50] And you're going in here to these groups and you're just making posts about your job opportunities, okay? And you're fielding reps. Let's go through some stats real quick. You'll probably have 90 plus% of these people just suck ass. Like they just won't be good. Okay? You'll have a lot more people that are going to come through that are like from foreign countries.
[35:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Facebook Group Recruitment Stats"
NÚMEROS: 90%+ will suck, majority from foreign countries
VISUAL: Funil de recrutamento mostrando: muitos aplicantes → filtros necessários → poucos qualificados, gráfico de pizza mostrando distribuição de qualidade
[35:20] And listen, after you make your first job post, you can refine it a bit and know what you need to post. So, as an example, and I have a whole video on this I've talked about, but again, I'll go through it here with you guys to be clear. When you make one of these posts, what you're doing is you're openly saying like, "Okay, so first of all, I'm looking for top closers that want to make 20 to 50k a month." That way, right off the bat, you're communicating to the right people.
[35:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Job Post Template Structure"
NÚMEROS: $20K-$50K/month target
VISUAL: Template estruturado em seções numeradas: 1) Target income, 2) No sales agencies, 3) Location requirements, 4) Offer description, 5) Pricing, 6) Commission, 7) Expectations, 8) CTA
[35:55] Then you specifically right after that want to say no sales agencies. Okay? And then from there, if you want a US-based rep or wherever you want the rep to be from, you want to define the location must be from blank. Okay? I typically do not work with the sales reps that are, as an example, from the US, but like internationally traveling.
[36:20] There's generally no serious people that are internationally traveling and working at an extremely high level at the same time. Especially when it comes to sales reps, sales reps, that creates a lot of time zone conflict as well. So, if I have some fucking dude in Bali that is going to try to take calls in a US time zone, they're on the other side of the earth. Okay? Their nighttime is the daytime in the US.
[36:45] It's like, and again, anytime somebody's over there in Bali, they're fucking off and doing lifestyle stuff all day and not making you money. Okay? And you think they're just going to stay up all night and take calls. It's like, no, you get like a fraction of these people's unfocused time. We want focused time. Focused time on the calendars, okay?
[37:10] It's like, take Leo. Leo's a perfect example. Leo's got four fucking kids, okay? Like you think Leo's over here going to fuck off in Bali and go drink coconuts on the beach and like do fuck all, you know? It's like no, dude. Leo's over here trying to put his kids through fucking college. Okay. Leo's over here trying to eventually retire, I'm sure, in like 20 years from now. You know, it's like Leo's trying to get himself into a financially secure position to level up his family's life.
[37:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Digital Nomad Problem"
NÚMEROS: 4 kids (Leo's example)
VISUAL: Comparação visual - Digital Nomad em Bali (unfocused, timezone conflicts, lifestyle distractions) vs. Stable Rep (Leo: 4 kids, college savings, retirement planning, family focus, making money)
[37:45] It's like, what do you think Leo's doing all day? Making fucking sales so he gets paid. You see what I mean? Making money. It's like again I'm in that post immediately saying like must be US-based because that's where I like my reps from. Again, you could plug wherever you want. No international traveling, none of that. Like we don't want that.
[38:10] And then just to start describe the offer itself. So from there it's like you want to describe and be very very descriptive like our offer is blank. You know, again, just be descriptive about it. It's okay to share this kind of information out there. And then just say price points range from this to this.
[38:30] And then remember the things that top closers want. Ideally, they want full calendars. Okay? So, if you're going to give them leads, that's okay. You can straight up tell them like, "Hey, we're going to fill your calendar, but—and then this is where you set the expectations. You'll be required to follow up with people. You'll be required to update the CRM. You'll be required to participate in sales training calls. You'll be required to like share your call recordings and get critiqued. You know, like this is where you start to describe A player culture.
[39:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Job Post: Setting Expectations"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Lista de requirements destacada: Full calendars provided, Follow-ups required, CRM updates mandatory, Training calls required, Call recordings reviewed, A-player culture defined
[39:10] So, after you describe the offer, after you describe the pricing of your offer, by the way, I missed a step. You want to also describe commission. So, right after you describe the offer, list off what you're actually going to pay people in terms of percentages and things like that. If you're doing this for setters, same thing. Just articulate what the setter pay is.
[39:35] Then you go through expectations. So the expectations meaning we're going to load up your calendars, you're going to do this, you're going to do this, you're going to show up to these, you know, like we have this kind of culture. That's what you're describing in those final steps. And then you make a call to action.
[39:50] So here's the thing. When you post inside of these Facebook groups, you're just going to get a bunch of people that comment. Okay? You want to have like a step above that. Like ideally, you say something like, "Message me a short video about why it should be you." Okay? This is one of the easiest ways to rapidly vet through reps.
[40:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Vetting Process: Video Filter"
NÚMEROS: 90%+ filtered out immediately
VISUAL: Funil de vetting: Post → Comments (ignore) → Video DMs (review) → Gut check → Interview questions → Final candidates
[40:20] You'll get a bunch of shit that just like comment something on the post and don't even take the action that you articulated they need to take. Discard those people straight away. From there, what you want to do is you want to go a little step further, right? You're going to have to sit there and watch a few messages in your Messenger and you're just going to go into Messenger and you're going to watch a few videos and just trust your gut.
[40:45] There's a bunch of people that will send you videos that right away you'll be like, "That's not a good one." Like I'm just going to ignore that and just feel free to just again like discard people as they come along that just aren't a good fit. For the ones that do have good videos from here, you just want to have a series of interview based questions.
[41:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Interview Questions Checklist"
NÚMEROS: $2M+/month (example flex stat from closer)
VISUAL: Lista de perguntas essenciais: 1) Current offer?, 2) Why leaving?, 3) What are you looking for?, 4) How fast can you start?, 5) How long can you train before taking calls? (financial stability indicator)
[41:15] Again, these should be like easily typed out or like easily articulated questions that you can ask one of these reps. So, from here it's like my first question is generally what offer are you on right now? Generally, what all the top reps are going to do is they're going to flex some kind of statistics on you about like their close rate or how much revenue they've driven.
[41:35] Generally, it's going to be some fucking audacious number like a couple million dollars. Sometimes you might even know the offer owner for whoever they're specifically working with. You can reach out, by the way, and you can straight up ask them like, "Hey, like what's up with this closer? Are they good? Are they bad?"
[41:55] We had a closer one time, as an example, that came through one of these groups. He had done about $2-something million dollars a month for the business. I knew the guy. I reached out and I was like, "Hey, like what's up with this guy? Why is he quitting your business?" You know, and the guy was like, "Dude, every person that guy's closed has fucking refunded. Like, it's insane. We had to fire him, you know?"
[42:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Due Diligence: Reference Check Example"
NÚMEROS: $2M+/month claimed revenue, 100% refund rate (reality)
VISUAL: Alerta vermelho: Stats flexing vs. Reality check, importância de verificar referências, exemplo de red flag (todos refundaram)
[42:25] It's like, "All right, well, obviously we don't want that rep." But that rep was flexing that he was doing $2 million a month for a competing business. So, it would have come off that it was a good, you know, good potential deal. Does that make sense to you? Like, you want to ask, okay, why are you leaving? Like, what's the current offer? Why are you leaving the current offer? What are you looking for out of working together? How fast can you start?
[42:55] And then there's also a very key question. This one's kind of a pro tip. You can tell where somebody's at financially based on how long they can endure a training window before officially taking calls themselves where they can get paid. Okay? So you specifically want to ask a more sophisticated question like how long are you able to train before taking calls.
[43:20] That question will reveal whether they're in like a desperate condition and that energy is going to come through on their sales calls with your business or whether they're in a position where they can really focus on learning your offer and mastering it and then getting on calls.
[43:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Training Window Financial Indicator"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Comparação: Short training tolerance = Desperate energy (bad) vs. Longer training capacity = Financial stability + Focus (good)
[43:42] Okay. When it comes to training of these people like after you officially onboard them, ideally just like what you guys experienced with me, ideally there's like a training library full of like call recordings that they can go through and that they can start to consume straight away. Ideally, you create like a training library that goes through your offer details. Like you essentially need your own version of a sales training library.
[44:10] There's a few things that I want to comment on here, okay? Like if you don't have these things, you're just going to have to manually do them and just make sure that you record this kind of stuff and then put it into a training library at a later date. That way you have an efficient systemized process that you can plug people into.
[44:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Training Library Components"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Checklist de biblioteca: Call recordings (good + bad examples), Offer details training, SOPs documented, Best practices, Word tracks, Key questions, Debrief processes
[44:35] If you guys have ever gone to a restaurant and they have like a new waiter or waitress, they don't just send them over to your table. They send it over with an already veteran waiter or waitress and they kind of tag team the table. Generally, when the new waiter or waitress is like literally fresh, like brand new, they're not going to say anything. They're just going to be behind the main veteran who's going to do their normal job and they're just sitting there passively learning.
[45:05] Once that trainee thinks that they know enough to officially switch the role, that's all that happens. The veteran officially stops talking and lets the trainee do their thing and the veteran observes when they're officially ready to go out on their own. That same exact process is the best thing to do with reps.
[45:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tag Team Training Process"
NÚMEROS: 3 phases
VISUAL: 3 fases ilustradas: PHASE 1 - Trainee silent, Veteran leads (no commission for trainee); PHASE 2 - Trainee leads, Veteran observes/saves (split commission); PHASE 3 - Trainee solo (full commission)
[45:30] Okay? You bring the reps into the business, you get them onto a tag teamed call where they just sit back and shut up. They just sit back and learn. They ask you questions afterwards. You do like a debrief call right after the call hangs up so they can go through their questions with you of why, what happened, and yada yada.
[45:50] From there, and this is very important to understand, you eventually have a period where the trainee is going to say, "Hey, I feel confident enough to officially take calls." Okay? And then that role just switches. That's where the trainee is officially the one that's leading the call. And the veteran rep is kicking back, just observing, and occasionally stepping in when necessary.
[46:15] Okay? That way, you're not blowing sales. The veteran rep is there to essentially save the deal when the trainee inevitably fucks up the first few calls that they take. Okay? And then eventually the veteran, this is the most important part of this, it's not the trainee that decides from here, it's the veteran that decides this person's officially good. They can go on their own.
[46:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Commission Split During Training"
NÚMEROS: 0% trainee (phase 1), 50/50 split (phase 2), 100% trainee (phase 3)
VISUAL: Estrutura de comissão progressiva, incentivo para veteran continuar treinando (getting paid), proteção de deals (veteran saves), decisão final com veteran (não trainee)
[46:45] Now, what you do during that time frame is you split the commission. So, as an example, the veteran is incentivized to lead the calls. The trainee gets no commission at all when it's the trainee doing the following. Okay. When the trainee officially takes over the calls and the veteran is there on those calls or the sales manager is on those calls, they're splitting the commission.
[47:10] So, the veteran still gets paid for spending that time on the training call without even really having to do much. They pretty much just sit around and observe and they're getting paid for it. Okay. At a high level, this is the sales management doing this. At a smaller level, these are the reps themselves, the veteran reps themselves that are on these calls. Okay? Or even you as the business owner. Does that make sense?
[47:35] Okay, let's go through a few more recruitment things. So, to be clear, on my end, I have a great network, okay, of sales reps that follow me. Why? It's not because I have a bigger following than you, okay? It's not because I make sales training content and post it on my Instagram page. It's because I post about closer opportunities.
[48:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Building Your Closer Network"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente
VISUAL: Network effect ilustrado: Post opportunity → Gets shared → More closers follow → Bigger network → Next post reaches more → Cycle continues, crescimento exponencial
[48:05] Okay? So, like you've got to start the process of just posting closer opportunities on your page and there's a network effect that occurs. So, even if you've got like a literal handful of closers that currently follow you, when you make a very simple post like you guys have seen me do on my stories, okay, that just says top closers wanted, no sales agencies, here's some brief details about the offer. Send me a video on why it should be you.
[48:35] Okay, what happens is that post gets shared a lot. Like, you're going to see a lot of people sharing those posts. At a smaller level of following, obviously your network effect is not going to be as extreme, but to be clear, it's like you'll still get a great network effect, and every time you do it, you grow your network effect to that next level.
[48:55] Okay? And then one thing that's important to do, and this is an undermined tactic, is you ideally are posting about like sales opportunities and just sales content in general. So like on my YouTube channel as an example, I have this entire 4 hour and 49 minute video which is just a sales training class. Okay? I didn't do this for any reason other than I wanted closers to follow me. So when I post and need closers, I have them there readily available. That's it. That's all I did that for.
[49:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Strategy For Closer Network"
NÚMEROS: 4 hours 49 minutes (training video example)
VISUAL: Content funnel: Sales training content → Attracts closers → They follow → Post opportunities → Ready network, tipos de content: YouTube training, Instagram clips, Stories with opportunities
[49:30] Okay. It's great to have content that can be clipped from that posted to my Instagram to also attract more closers. Okay? It's not my prerogative with my Instagram account to just post exclusively to build a closer network. But again, like once you actually get into the game here a little bit and you start, okay, there's a few things.
[49:50] Once you start posting in those Facebook groups, once you start posting like content for closers, once you start posting on your stories for like closer opportunities, those are the main things that start to create like a really strong closer network effect. They'll just follow you and wait for more opportunities. They'll share them with their friends.
[50:15] And this is this final point I want to make here. This is important to understand. Network effect. So, if you already know a good closer, if you already know like anybody, not even people who work for you to be clear, just anybody who's a good closer, you typically, those are going to be some of the best people you can also leverage and lean into where you just ask, hey, do you know any good closers right now looking for a role?
[50:40] You know, I'm looking to get somebody 20 to 50K a month. Kick them my way and just have them shoot me a DM. You know, don't make it too complex. Don't add too much friction. Just literally ask your network and the people that you know that are good closers for these opportunities. Does that make sense?
[51:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Leverage Your Network"
NÚMEROS: $20K-$50K/month opportunity
VISUAL: Network referral system: Your network → Ask for referrals → Good closers know good closers → Direct DM (low friction), exemplo: Kaden & Trey têm reps disponíveis
[51:05] Okay. A good example of this, by the way, is like, "How often do you guys see me in the chat ask Kaden and Trey," hey, you guys have a rep for this guy? You know, it's like they generally have some kind of rep just sitting there chilling that they can't use in that exact moment that they can kick your way. You know, it's a good example of the network effect that I'm talking about. You understand?
[51:30] Okay. Anybody have questions on this? Unmute yourself and ask away if you do. Anybody else have other strategies that you find beneficial you want to share? By the way,
[51:42] "I have a side question. Could you do a call on like redesigning offers where you can charge that amount because I think there's a lot that goes into that more than just being like, 'Yeah, I'm going to charge more.'"
[51:55] Yeah. Watch my latest YouTube video where I just talked about offer creation. I was just talking about this with Justin Waller the other day and I made a whole video on it. It's literally the most recent video called the offer creation formula.
[52:10] "Got it."
[52:12] And then follow up with me on questions you have after that. Okay, cool. Other questions on this.
[52:18] "I was going to share my one strategy that's been really helpful for me is making friends with people like Kaden or people who run these sales communities because yeah, whenever I've got a position, I can share it with them and they'll share it with like 50 people and it's helped my hit rate a lot."
[52:38] Yeah, perfect. Makes a lot of sense. And that's that network effect, you know. I love the network effect. It makes a lot of sense to have that. And keep in mind like sure anytime that they have closers available and they share them with us, you know, be grateful for that opportunity, but there's plenty of ways to farm people outside of that, especially from those groups I just talked about.
[53:05] You know, the other ones after the groups are more long-term, you know, like you can still post on your stories and get a strong response. You can still go out there and like start posting some closer content here and there that just gets closers to watch your page and like understand that you're making content for them too. But at the end of the day, like yeah, just knowing people sometimes has the best result. So, good idea.
[53:30] Okay, cool. Other questions. All right, sweet. All right, guys. Well, if you got anything else, let me know. Again, go out there and remember the number one thing, and this is the most important thing. When you have a group of people that want to make 20 to 50K a month, and it prompts you to start making those changes in your business to make room for those people, pay really close attention to those specific changes that you need to make and start to adjust the business to compensate for these people.
[54:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Key Takeaway: Business Transformation"
NÚMEROS: $20K-$50K/month target drives all changes
VISUAL: Ciclo de transformação: Top rep requirements → Force business improvements → Higher pricing → Better offers → More revenue → Enriches everyone → Attracts more A-players
[54:10] So the business enriches everybody and doesn't just become a business that's like, you know, something that you've essentially selfishly cash flowed on. Once you have the capacity in an organization to start paying people consistently like that level of income, dude, I mean, that's when your business just pushes the fuck up rapidly in revenue is when you really take that seriously more than anything else.
[54:35] You know, how can I create an organization that is going to pay everybody a part of the organization what their dream revenue is? Does that make sense? That's like one of my favorite things to consider whenever I'm considering hiring top closers. It's like I love that opportunity because again like that lead if they're making that amount, what's the company making, you know?
[55:00] All right, cool. You guys got followup questions? Let me know. All right.
[55:05] "Thanks, Jeremy."
[55:07] "Thank you, Jeremy. Appreciate you, man."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Top closers querem fazer $40K-$50K/mês (não $10K-$20K)
Leo faz ~$35K/mês após uma década de trabalho
30,000 milhas rodadas em carro da empresa em 2 anos (Leo)
90%+ dos aplicantes de Facebook groups não servem
500 calls = threshold de confiança para contratar reps
7-8 calls/dia durante 6 meses antes de contratar (exemplo Andrew)
Cole Gordon: 99% dos compradores não faziam $10K/mês (dado FTC)
$15K cobrado do business + $2K do closer (modelo Cole Gordon)
Cliente Ambrose: $500K/mês com 4 closers (poderia ser $1M com 8)
Contratos de 2 anos (Ambrose)
$1,600 → $10K-$15K necessário para pricing viável (exemplo Max)
$1,800/mês → $4,000 (2 semanas) → $8K-$12K (evolução de pricing)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Estrutura de Compensação:
Target: $20K-$50K/mês para top closers
Comissões estruturadas para viabilizar esse range
Split commission durante treinamento (0% → 50/50 → 100%)
Sistema de Recrutamento:
Facebook groups (Dan Lock, job boards, closer communities)
Post structure: Target income → No agencies → Location → Offer → Pricing → Commission → Expectations → CTA
Video filter: "Send me video why it should be you"
Interview questions essenciais
Reference checks obrigatórios
Processo de Treinamento (Tag Team):
Fase 1: Trainee silent, Veteran leads (trainee 0% commission)
Fase 2: Trainee leads, Veteran saves (50/50 split)
Fase 3: Trainee solo (100% commission)
Veteran decide quando trainee está pronto
Sistema Provado Antes de Contratar:
Você faz 500+ calls primeiro
Documenta o que funciona/não funciona
Cria biblioteca de call recordings
Desenvolve SOPs e best practices
Word tracks e key questions documentados
Depois pluga reps no sistema
Network Effect Strategy:
Post closer opportunities regularmente
Criar conteúdo de sales training (YouTube, Instagram)
Leverage network existente (perguntar referrals)
Posts são compartilhados = network cresce
ESTRUTURA:
O Que Top Closers Querem:
Money ($40K-$50K/mês)
Personal Development (accountability, routine, growth)
Shielded from business owner stress
Full calendars/leads provided
Proven system (não caos)
Proper sales management
A-player culture
Development programs
O Que Top Closers NÃO Querem:
Business owner stress
Uncertainty/chaos
Undefined systems
International travel requirements (timezone conflicts)
Lifestyle distractions
Desperate financial position
Problemas com Sales Agencies:
Cole Gordon: Scale problems, mediocre talent ($10K target atrai mediocres), no proper management, swap infinito de reps ruins
Ambrose: Contratos 2 anos, não compartilha call recordings, não compartilha CRM data, no statistics, hold businesses back intencionalmente
Incentivos errados: quanto maior ficam, pior performance
Solução: In-house sales team
In-House Sales Team Benefits:
Você aprende o sistema primeiro
Cria biblioteca de training
Proper onboarding estruturado
Management direto e accountability
Pode escalar sem limites artificiais
Reps desenvolvem dentro do seu sistema
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Viabilidade Financeira:
Pricing precisa suportar $20K-$50K/mês em commissions
Exemplo: $1,600 pricing = inviável → precisa $10K-$15K
Mudanças necessárias: Demographics (mais ricos), Messaging, Conversion mechanism, Deliverables
ROI de Top Reps:
Se rep faz $50K/mês, company faz múltiplos disso
Leo exemplo: 10 anos, consistente $35K/mês, 4 kids suportados, aposentadoria sendo construída
Company fornece: carro (30K milhas em 2 anos), viagens, vacations, rewards
Investimento em Sistema:
Tempo: 500 calls (~2-3 meses) antes de contratar
Training library creation
Sales management structure
CRM e sistemas de accountability
Modelo de Crescimento:
Mais reps = mais revenue exponencial
Exemplo cliente Ambrose: 4 closers = $500K/mês, 8 closers = $1M/mês potencial
Cada rep bem pago atrai mais A-players
Organization enriches everyone = rapid revenue growth
DURAÇÃO: ~55 minutos FORMATO: Masterclass/Training ao vivo com Q&A, exemplos de membros (Leo, Andrew, Max, Euan), comparações de modelos de sales agencies
